output_summary: aligned schemas for datasets: dbpedia, metacritic, sales; generated 2 schema correspondence groups.
profiled datasets: dbpedia, metacritic, sales.
Reused the blocking configuration from state.
Blocking uses `semantic_similarity` for dbpedia_sales, metacritic_dbpedia, metacritic_sales, blocking on name, platform, releaseYear.
Performance-wise, pair completeness ranges from 0.91 to 1.00 and candidate counts range from 409504 to 929573.
The config also records dataset id columns.

matcher mode `ml` with config source reused from state; evaluated pairs: id_columns, matching_strategies; selected scores: matching_strategies (threshold=0.75, f1=0.9973474801061007, f1=0.9944444444444445, f1=1.0); produced 2 pair strategy entries.
This run produced an initial draft of the pipeline.
The pipeline was created to load three schema-matched CSV datasets for dbpedia, metacritic, and sales and process all three pairwise matching tasks.
It added three EmbeddingBlocker instances with top_k set to 20 for dbpedia-sales, metacritic-dbpedia, and metacritic-sales using different text column combinations per pair.

It configured pair-specific comparator sets that combine string, date, and numeric similarity features and used FeatureExtractor objects to build training features from provided pairwise testset files.

It introduced model selection via GridSearchCV over RandomForest, LogisticRegression, GradientBoosting, and SVM classifiers with F1 scoring and stratified 5-fold cross-validation for each dataset pair.

It applied MLBasedMatcher for each pair, clustered the resulting correspondences with MaximumBipartiteMatching, saved each correspondence file under the required output names, concatenated all correspondences, and defined a fusion strategy using longest_string for 10 attributes.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 1; fusion_data.csv was produced.
This run was an initial draft of the evaluation script.
The evaluation was built around aligning fused rows to gold record ids before running DataFusionEvaluator with field-specific matching functions.
The strategy combined explicit per-column evaluators for core game fields with automatic set-based evaluation for detected list-like columns.
Name, developer, platform, series, publisher, and ESRB were grouped under tokenized_match evaluation.
releaseYear was evaluated with year_only_match.
criticScore, userScore, and globalSales were grouped under numeric_tolerance_match.
Detected list-like non-underscore columns were grouped under set_equality_match after normalization across fused and gold dataframes.
The run added build_eval_view to expand fused rows by parsed _fusion_sources, prefer gold-prefixed or gold-present source ids, and fall back to cluster _id when no mapping candidates existed.
The run added _parse_source_ids to parse serialized source-id collections using json.loads and ast.literal_eval, with safe empty-list fallbacks.
The run added _detect_gold_prefix to infer the dominant gold id prefix for source-id matching.
evaluation execution succeeded on attempt 1; pipeline_evaluation.json is available.
overall accuracy is 78.99%; evaluation attempt counter is 1.
The run is failing primarily because the fusion stage selects wrong attribute values inside matched clusters, not because blocking or pairwise matching is poor.
A secondary problem is occasional cluster contamination or over-merged entities that allow values from the wrong linked record to be fused into the final output.
Overall accuracy is 0.7899159663865546 and macro accuracy is 0.7851190476190477 across 119 evaluated attributes from 15 records.
Blocking pair completeness is reported as high to perfect at 0.91, 0.96, and 1.0, while matching F1 is reported as 0.997, 0.994, and 1.0.
Attribute errors are concentrated in fusion outputs, especially userScore accuracy at 0.21428571428571427, developer accuracy at 0.7333333333333333, and criticScore accuracy at 0.8.
Platform accuracy is 1.0, which supports that core grouping is often good while selected fused values are wrong.
The current pipeline applies longest_string as the fuser for name, releaseYear, developer, platform, series, publisher, criticScore, userScore, and ESRB.

Using longest_string on numeric and date-like fields such as criticScore, userScore, globalSales, and releaseYear treats typed values as strings and can systematically select noncanonical or incorrect values.
This run was a refinement in evaluation_refinement mode focused on correcting fusion behavior after evaluation exposed weak attribute value selection.

The pipeline removed longest_string fusion from all fused attributes and introduced attribute-specific helper fusers for name, releaseYear, developer, publisher, platform, ESRB, series, and numeric fields.

The fusion logic shifted to source-priority selection, especially preferring metacritic for name, criticScore, userScore, ESRB, platform, and releaseYear, while preferring sales for publisher and globalSales and dbpedia for series.
The pipeline added preprocessing and canonicalization for titles, company names, ESRB values, dates, and numeric parsing before fusion.
The run added correspondence filtering through drop_suspicious_correspondences to remove likely contaminated matches based on title, platform, and releaseYear agreement.
The fusion strategy moved away from blind longest-string selection toward typed, attribute-aware resolution rules.
Matching and blocking were not the main target of change, and the refinement instead emphasized trusted-source selection and cluster cleanliness during fusion.

This run was a repair of the pipeline after a previous execution failure caused by a syntax error.
The run replaced generic longest-string fusion with attribute-specific fusers for name, releaseYear, developer, publisher, platform, series, criticScore, userScore, ESRB, and globalSales.

The run added source-priority fusion behavior that prefers metacritic for name, criticScore, userScore, ESRB, releaseYear, developer, and platform, while preferring sales for publisher and globalSales.
The run introduced typed fusion for numeric and date fields through numeric parsing, median fallback, and date parsing instead of string-length selection.
The run added correspondence filtering through drop_suspicious_correspondences to remove suspicious matches based on title, platform, and release-year agreement.
The fusion strategy shifted from blind longest-string selection to source-aware and type-aware value selection.
The matching and fusion behavior shifted toward trusting metacritic-centric values for key identity and score attributes.
The run also shifted cluster handling toward rejecting potentially contaminated links when names, platforms, or dates disagree too strongly.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 2; fusion_data.csv was produced.
This run was an evaluation refinement rather than an initial draft or repair.
The dominant strategy was to refine evaluation by registering attribute-specific comparison functions only for columns present in both fused and gold data.
The run kept tokenized matching for text-like fields, year-only matching for releaseYear, and numeric tolerance matching for criticScore and userScore.
Text identity and categorical fields were grouped under tokenized_match, including name, developer, publisher, platform, and ESRB.
Temporal fields were grouped under year_only_match for releaseYear.
Numeric score fields were grouped under numeric_tolerance_match for criticScore and userScore.
List-like columns detected from the XML gold set were evaluated with tokenized_match when they existed in both datasets.
Fields not shared by both fused and gold schemas were excluded from evaluation registration, including prior unconditional coverage such as series and globalSales.
The run removed set_equality_match from imports and replaced the prior list-like fallback with tokenized_match guarded by schema presence checks.
The evaluation strategy name was changed to ml_fusion_strategy_eval to reflect a schema-aware evaluation configuration.
evaluation execution succeeded on attempt 1; pipeline_evaluation.json is available.
overall accuracy is 82.35%; previous logged accuracy was 78.99%; evaluation attempt counter is 2.
The run is mainly failing in fusion value selection rather than in blocking or matching.
The dominant attribute failure is userScore, where the fusion logic often chooses the wrong value due to brittle hard-coded source priority.
Secondary problems come from over-aggressive normalization and some noisy correspondences inside otherwise matched clusters.
Overall accuracy is 0.8235294117647058 with 98 correct out of 119 evaluations, while userScore accuracy is only 0.21428571428571427 with 3 correct out of 14 and 11 mismatches.
Blocking and matching are already strong, with blocking pair completeness reported as 0.91, 0.96, and 1.00, and matching F1 reported as 0.994, 0.997, and 1.00.

The pipeline code shows hard-coded source-priority fusion, including fuser_numeric_prefer_metacritic for userScore and criticScore, fuser_release_year preferring metacritic_, fuser_developer preferring metacritic_, fuser_publisher preferring sales_, and fuser_esrb preferring metacritic_.
This run was a refinement in evaluation_refinement mode driven by the latest error analysis.
The pipeline replaced source-priority numeric fusion for userScore and criticScore with new robust numeric fusers named fuser_user_score and fuser_critic_score.
The releaseYear fusion was rewritten from source-priority date selection to a year-based consensus rule that counts candidate years, resolves ties conservatively, and outputs a normalized year date.
The developer and publisher fusion logic was changed to preserve original surface forms while grouping by normalized company variants through choose_surface_form.
The ESRB handling was split into matching-time and output-time canonicalization, and output fusion now preserves labels such as M17+ instead of collapsing them to M.
Text fusion helpers were changed from choose_by_priority to choose_text_majority for fields such as name, platform, series, and ESRB.
Fusion shifted away from blindly trusting preferred sources, especially Metacritic, toward majority, consensus, and outlier-aware selection.
Numeric fusion now clusters close values within a tolerance and uses cluster consensus or medians before falling back to source preference.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 1; fusion_data.csv was produced.
This run was an evaluation refinement rather than an initial draft or repair.
The dominant strategy was to refine evaluation around fusion-value selection errors instead of blocking or matcher failures.
The analysis prioritized field-specific evaluation behavior, with numeric tolerance checks for scores, year-only checks for releaseYear, and tokenized matching for string attributes.
Numeric fields were treated as the main failure family, with userScore as the dominant error source and criticScore as a secondary issue.
Temporal evaluation centered on releaseYear, where the recommended behavior was a conservative consensus or majority-year interpretation rather than rigid source-priority selection.
Textual organization fields including developer and publisher were grouped as conflict-prone fusion outputs affected by source preference, canonicalization, and possible noisy correspondences.
Categorical normalization-sensitive fields such as ESRB were grouped separately because evaluation was being distorted by over-collapsing labels that should remain distinct.
Stable families such as name and platform were effectively strong already and were not identified as primary evaluation bottlenecks.
evaluation execution succeeded on attempt 1; pipeline_evaluation.json is available.
